one_line_takeaway: A first SoK survey of MCP security/safety whose real contribution is a unified taxonomy separating adversarial security threats from epistemic safety hazards and a defense-in-depth roadmap; it presents no original experiments and all its numbers are borrowed from cited work.
key_finding: MCP's defining risk is the collapse of the security/safety distinction: an injected instruction in a low-trust Resource can escalate into a real-world destructive Tool action (cross-primitive escalation), so containment must be architectural. The paper's illustrative anchor is the 2025 Supabase incident: an attacker embedded a hidden directive in a support-ticket message; when a developer later asked an AI assistant (Claude via Cursor IDE) to show the latest ticket, the LLM treated the attacker's data as a command and, holding a powerful service_role credential that bypassed row-level security, exfiltrated the integration_tokens table into the visible ticket thread. The authors generalize that HITL alone is inadequate and that defense requires layered controls (cryptographic tool provenance/ETDI, least-privilege capability tokens, prompt/context sanitization, ephemeral sandboxed sessions, and continuous monitoring), enforced via a tiered-trust model and a three-phase adoption roadmap (visibility/containment -> zero-trust/identity-aware proxies -> governance-as-code with watchdog agents).
core_contribution: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) survey of security and safety in the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem. The paper's own contributions are conceptual/organizational rather than empirical: (1) a unified vulnerability taxonomy (Table III) that explicitly separates adversarial SECURITY threats (context poisoning, prompt injection, unauthorized context injection, data leakage, cross-session contamination, supply-chain/model-switch, protocol abuse/name collisions, DoS) from epistemic SAFETY hazards (hallucination/grounding failures, adversarial steering, policy conflicts, HITL vulnerabilities, dual-use); (2) a security-vs-safety framing organized along Integrity/Confidentiality/Availability dimensions (Table II) and a mapping of safety challenges to the three MCP primitives Resources/Prompts/Tools (Table IV); (3) a structural analysis arguing that decoupling 'Context' (Resources) from 'Action' (Tools) creates new vulnerability classes such as 'Cross-Primitive Escalation' where low-trust read-only context weaponizes high-privilege write-capable tools; (4) a synthesis of emerging defenses (ETDI signed tool manifests, capability-bound execution, prompt sanitization/DDG provenance, seL4/gVisor session isolation, TRiSM closed-loop governance) into a defense-in-depth control plane; and (5) forensic case-study reconstructions (notably the Supabase confused-deputy leak) plus a tiered-trust adoption roadmap. The central thesis is that MCP dissolves the traditional security/safety boundary and therefore requires a unified threat model treating context availability and execution privilege as inextricably linked.
threat_model: The paper's subject IS a threat model, so this is the paper's own framing, not an invented one. Adversaries: malicious MCP-server developers (tool squatting, 'rug-pull' behavior), supply-chain attackers (tampered installers, rogue updates, malicious packages in open registries akin to npm/PyPI), insider threats abusing over-scoped/misconfigured tools, and sophisticated actors exploiting weak authentication, over-privileged tools, and absent integrity checks to achieve RCE, token theft, and lateral movement. Core attack surface is interpretive rather than code-level: MCP embeds semantic intent into the connection so 'context' can be weaponized. Primary vectors: indirect/tool prompt injection (malicious instructions hidden in Resources, docstrings, tool descriptions, or retrieved content), context poisoning and cross-context contamination, server shadowing/model-switch (spoofed servers mimicking trusted ones), and crafted JSON-RPC protocol abuse. Trust boundary: the host application is nominally the security/policy-enforcement point, and Human-in-the-Loop approval is often the single line of defense; the paper argues this is insufficient because firewalls cannot inspect JSON-RPC semantic intent and LLM safety filters cannot see downstream tool-execution consequences. Distributed topology (independent Hosts/Clients/Servers, possibly across different providers) means a single compromised endpoint cascades and makes accountability/liability propagation unclear.
limitations: No original empirical evaluation: the paper is a synthesis, so it validates none of the surveyed defenses (ETDI, MindGuard/DDG, gVisor/seL4 isolation, TRiSM) with its own experiments or measurements. Every quantitative claim is inherited from cited sources of varying rigor, and the survey leans heavily on non-peer-reviewed industry blog posts and vendor reports (Elena Cross, Forge Code, General Analysis, Piazza, Traub) alongside academic work, so evidence quality is uneven. Several referenced artifacts (MCP-AttackBench 70k samples, MCPTox, MCP-Guard, ScanMCP) are described secondhand without independent verification. The taxonomies are conceptual and not empirically validated against a corpus of real MCP servers by the authors themselves. Some forward-looking sections (AI-native OS 'kernel', watchdog agents, multi-modal sensory injection) are speculative. The self-described 'Sources Note' acknowledges the content is synthesized from mixed sources.

quantitative_results: Over 43% of MCP server implementations tested by Equixly were found to execute unsafe shell calls, enabling remote code execution (cited from source [2], not measured by the authors)
MCP-AttackBench is described as containing 70k adversarial samples for red-teaming (cited from source [47], not run by the authors)
LongSafety benchmark evaluates LLM behavior on ~40k-token contexts (cited from Huang et al. 2024 [49], not run by the authors)

Supabase case study: attacker exfiltrated the sensitive integration_tokens table using service_role credentials that bypassed row-level security, via a prompt injected into a support ticket read by Claude in Cursor IDE (reconstructed from incident post-mortems [8],[50], not an authors' experiment)
